---
title: "Sarc draft results"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = here::here())

library(flextable)
library(tidyverse)

e_table <- readRDS(here::here("data-generated", "summary-encounter-table.rds"))
n_all_samples <- sum(c(e_table$`0`, e_table$`1`))

d <- readRDS(here::here("data-generated", "clean-data.rds"))
ld <- readRDS(here::here("data-generated", "length-dat.rds"))

```

## Summary results

### Spatial distribution of occurrence

Years included in the spatial analysis included 2019 - 2022 only because in 1999 and 2000 sarc occurrence data were only collected in `r d |> filter(year %in% 1999:2000) |> distinct(fishing_event_id) |> nrow()` tows.

### Species infection rates
- Table 1 summarises all the data: total number of records = `r scales::comma(n_all_samples)`
- Only positive sarc encounters were recorded in most years.
- A few species were only opportunisitically sampled when infected with Sarcotaces: Sharpchin Rockfish, Darkblotched Rockfish, Dusky Rockfish, and Rosethorn Rockfish.
- Six species were sampled and never found to be infected with Sarcs (grey in Table 1). Of these, Shortbelly Rockfish, Tiger Rockfish, Pygmy Rockfish, and Black Rockfish have few samples (< 175). Given that the lowest observed infection rate of rockfish is < 1% (e.g., 1 / 292 = 0.34% China rockfish), these are probably not well sampled enough to have detections.
- The species that were not systematically documented to be infections (i.e., uninfected tallied) were excluded from the rest of the analyses.
- Shortspine Thornyhead had a high sampling rate (n = 1202) yet no detections. 
- Longspine Thornyhead had 592 samples with no infections.

```{r}

e_table |>
  mutate(Species = stringr::str_to_title(species), 
         n = `0` + `1`, 
         p = round(100 * (`1` / n), digits = 1)
         ) |>
  arrange(sarc_group, -p, -`1`, -`0`) |>
  mutate(p = ifelse(`0` == 0, "-", as.character(p))) |>
  select(Species, Uninfected = `0`, Infected = `1`, n, `Prevalence (%)` = p) |>
  flextable::flextable() |>
  bg(i = ~ Infected == 0, bg = "grey90") |>
  align(j = "Prevalence (%)", align = "right", part = "all") |>
  flextable::set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") |>
  flextable::set_caption(
    caption = "Table 1. Sample sizes and infection detections for species examined for Sarcotaces from 1969 - 2022. Species highlighted in grey had no observed infections."
  )

```

### Depth distribution

Figure/set_caption notes:

Depth distribution of Sarcotaces sp. observations within species. 
Species are arranged from left to right based on the absolute number of infected individuals. 
The estimated density of uninfected (grey) and infected (white) fish is displayed, excluding cases with fewer than two observations. 
Short horizontal lines represent individual observations.

- Note: some species had zero depth values, I didn't filter these out because I wasn't sure if we wanted to have a visual of all the occurrences instead of a table, but maybe with table one these could be filtered out. We could also opt to show only species with more than X infections. I included them all for now.
- In total there were `r sum(d$depth == 0)` depths = 0

```{r}
# No idea why I need this: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/75458365/knitrinclude-graphics-cant-find-file-if-knitr-root-dir-is-changed
knitr::include_graphics(path = normalizePath("figures/depth-plot.png"), rel_path = FALSE)

```

### Encounter rates

- Encounter data were only systematically sampled in six years (1999-2000 and 2019-2022).
- Thought encounter data would be helpful for 


### Maturity ~ length 

- comparison of parameter posterior densities from models that included Sarc presence (Infection) or Sarc count

```{r}
table(ld$sarc_count) |>
  enframe() |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = name, values_from = value) |>
  flextable::flextable() |>
  flextable::set_caption("Frequency table of Sarcs found per specimen")
```


```{r}

knitr::include_graphics(path = normalizePath("figures/maturity-length-infection-count-posterior-density.png"), 
  rel_path = FALSE)

```

<br> </br>

Mature ~ Length * Infection (1 | species)

```{r}

knitr::include_graphics(path = normalizePath("figures/maturity-length-infection-by-species.png"), 
  rel_path = FALSE)
```

<br> </br>

#### Maturity ~ length * sarc_count

Not really enough data to look at species effects of sarc count on maturity,
but I think there is enough to make a general statement about what it does on
average - that a higher intensity of infection does likely have a negative
effect on maturity at length/age
